metadata.summary: Modern JS/TS/Python standards 2026: TypeScript 5 strict, React 19 RSC, Next.js 15 caching, Python 3.13, ruff, AI-assisted dev guardrails.

content.title: Software Development Best Practices 2026
content.summary: Concrete 2026 baseline for TypeScript 5, React 19, Next.js 15, Python 3.13, and AI-assisted development. Provides triggers, checklists, paste-ready configs, and a verification procedure so an autonomous agent can audit or modernize a project without further guidance.

why.p: Stack currency directly affects build-time performance, type safety, and developer velocity. The 2026 baseline encodes which features are now default (TS strict mode, React server-first, ruff over flake8 + black + isort), which are adopted gradually (exactOptionalPropertyTypes), and which AI-tooling practices reduce rather than increase rework. Quarterly review against this hub prevents stack drift.

list.item: Auditing whether a project's stack aligns with current standards (TS version, eslint config, Python tooling).
Generating the standards section of CONTRIBUTING.md or AGENTS.md for a new project.
Choosing which React 19 / Next.js 15 / Python 3.13 features to adopt in a modernization sprint.
Defining an AI-assisted dev playbook: tool selection, prompt patterns, AI test generation guardrails.
Reviewing a PR that touches tsconfig.json, pyproject.toml, eslint config, package.json engines, or Next.js fetch calls.
Deep language-specific patterns (services, views, handlers) — see dev-methodologies-practices.
Architecture decisions (monolith vs microservice) — see dev-methodologies-architecture.
When the project has a stricter or newer house standard than this 2026 baseline — defer to that.
One-shot bug fixes — load only the specific section, not the full hub.

how-to.step: Detect the stack: read package.json (typescript, react, next versions) and pyproject.toml (requires-python, ruff config). Record the gap from each rule below.
Apply the TypeScript 5 strict tsconfig template; flip strict per-file via // @ts-strict-check before flipping globally.
Apply the Python 3.13 + ruff pyproject.toml template; run ruff check --fix and ruff format on the whole tree once.
Audit Next.js fetch() calls: ensure each one explicitly opts into cache: 'force-cache' or revalidate where caching is desired (Next 15 default is no-cache).
Audit React component files: enforce server-by-default; 'use client' must appear only at boundary files with a comment explaining why.
Audit AI-generated diffs: any single PR over 200 lines from an AI tool must be split or reviewed in two passes.
Run the verification procedure in <verify> and capture results into a markdown report under .aidocs/audits/<date>-best-practices-2026.md.

checklist.check: tsconfig.json contains: strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, noImplicitOverride, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, verbatimModuleSyntax.
tsconfig.json moduleResolution matches runtime: "bundler" for Vite/Next/tsup, "node16" or "nodenext" for pure Node.
All type-only imports use import type (verbatimModuleSyntax requirement).
package.json engines.node pins to a current LTS (>=20 minimum, >=22 preferred for 2026).
React 19 server components: 'use client' appears only at interactive leaf files; each occurrence has a one-line justification comment.
Next.js 15 fetch() audit: every fetch in app/ directory either has cache option or is explicitly documented as request-fresh.
Server Actions use 'use server' inside async functions; input is validated with zod or pydantic equivalent before any DB write.
pyproject.toml [tool.ruff.lint] select includes E, F, I, B, UP, SIM, T20, ASYNC at minimum.
No typing.List / typing.Dict / Optional[X] in new code — use list[X], dict[K,V], X | None (PEP 585 + PEP 604).
No print() in production Python (T20); use structlog/logging.
One primary AI coding tool selected and documented in AGENTS.md; not a mix of three.
Every AI-generated PR has a human-reviewed diff; PRs over 200 lines are split or get a second review pass.
AI-generated tests are mutation-tested: revert the change-under-test and confirm at least one new test fails.
Codemods (React 19, TS 5 upgrades) ran per-package with a verification step between packages, not repo-wide in one shot.
CHANGELOG.md or RELEASE_NOTES.md updated for any baseline change.

section.name: typescript-5-strict-configuration-2026-baseline
section.p: Recommended TS 5 strict flags, module resolution settings, and advanced type patterns for 2026.

rule.statement: Enable strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, noImplicitOverride, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, and verbatimModuleSyntax in new projects; migrate legacy code under per-file // @ts-strict-check before flipping the global flag.
rule.rationale: Flipping global strict in an existing codebase without budget creates hundreds of errors at once. Per-file gradual adoption lets the team absorb changes incrementally.

section.reference: Full TS 5 strict baseline with all 2026 recommended flags.

example.title: TypeScript 5 strict configuration
example.code: { "compilerOptions": { "target": "ES2023", "module": "ESNext", "moduleResolution": "bundler", "strict": true, "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true, "noImplicitOverride": true, "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true, "noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature": true, "verbatimModuleSyntax": true, "isolatedModules": true, "skipLibCheck": true } }

rule.statement: Use moduleResolution: "bundler" for projects targeting Vite, Next.js, or tsup; use "node16" or "nodenext" for pure Node projects without a bundler.
rule.rationale: "bundler" allows extensionless imports that bundlers resolve; "node16" requires .js extensions in import paths for native ESM compliance.

rule.statement: With verbatimModuleSyntax enabled, always use import type for type-only imports; the compiler will error on value imports that are erased at runtime.
rule.rationale: Prevents accidental inclusion of types-only modules in runtime bundles and makes the module graph explicit.

example.title: verbatimModuleSyntax import patterns
example.code: import type { User } from './types'; import { createUser } from './factory'; // value import — fine

section.name: react-19-next-js-15-python-3-13-2026-patterns
section.p: Server components/actions policy, Next.js 15 caching changes, Python 3.13 features, and ruff configuration.

rule.statement: Adopt server-components-by-default per route; add 'use client' only where interactivity is required — never sprinkle it at the module level without a documented per-route policy.
rule.rationale: Client boundaries prevent tree-shaking of server-only code; undisciplined use bloats the client bundle with dependencies that should never reach the browser.

rule.statement: Use Server Actions ('use server' in an async function) for form submissions and mutations; they run on the server, validate data there, and can access the database directly without an API route.
rule.rationale: Eliminates a client-to-API round-trip for simple mutations, removes the need to expose internal data shapes via an API, and colocates mutation logic with the form.

rule.statement: In Next.js 15, fetch() is no longer cached by default; explicitly opt in with cache: 'force-cache' or revalidate for data that should be cached.
rule.rationale: Next.js 15 reversed the default from cache-everything to no-cache. Code written for Next.js 14 that relies on implicit caching will fetch on every request in 15.

example.title: Next.js 15 explicit cache opt-in

rule.statement: Pin Python at >=3.13,<3.14 in pyproject.toml until the next minor LTS stabilises; configure ruff with target-version = "py313" and select E, F, I, B, UP, SIM, T20, ASYNC.
rule.rationale: >=3.13 without an upper bound accepts future majors and breaks compatibility. ruff replaces black + isort + flake8 in a single binary; T20 catches print() statements left in production code.

example.title: Python 3.13 pyproject.toml configuration
example.code:
[project] requires-python = ">=3.13,<3.14"
[tool.ruff] target-version = "py313" line-length = 100
[tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E","F","I","B","UP","SIM","N","TCH","T20","ASYNC"]
[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.13" strict = true plugins = ["pydantic.mypy"]

rule.statement: Use built-in generic types (list[str], dict[str, int], tuple[int, ...]) instead of typing.List, typing.Dict; use X | None instead of Optional[X].
rule.rationale: PEP 585 and PEP 604 make the old typing module aliases deprecated. Mixing old and new in the same file causes inconsistent style that UP ruff rules will flag.

section.name: ai-assisted-development-tool-selection-and-guardrails
section.p: Criteria for choosing AI coding tools, safe prompt patterns, and guardrails for AI-generated test suites.

rule.statement: Choose the AI coding tool based on primary workflow: Claude Code for terminal-first, multi-file refactors and agent tasks; Cursor for IDE-native pair programming with codebase context; GitHub Copilot for line-completion embedded in any editor.
rule.rationale: Tool capabilities overlap but their UX models differ; mixing all three without a clear primary creates context fragmentation and inconsistent code style.

rule.statement: Require diff review on every AI-generated change; reject any AI-produced diff over 200 lines unreviewed as a single unit.
rule.rationale: AI models have consistent blind spots in the same training domains. Large unreviewed diffs obscure subtle logic errors under volume. 200 lines is approximately one focused review session.

antipattern.description: Accepting AI-generated code in bulk without review because "it looks right".
antipattern.reason: AI tools produce plausible-looking code that may silently introduce security issues, wrong algorithms, or subtle type errors that only surface at runtime.

rule.statement: Run the original test suite to establish a baseline before generating tests with AI; reject AI-generated tests that do not fail when reverting the change they test.
rule.rationale: AI-generated tests for AI-generated code share the same blind spots. A test that does not fail on revert provides no actual protection and creates false confidence in coverage.

rule.statement: Use a different model or prompt context to review AI-generated tests than was used to generate the code under test.
rule.rationale: The same model asked to generate code and tests will produce tests that mirror the implementation's assumptions, not independently verify correctness.

rule.statement: Run automated codemods (React 19 migration, TS 5 upgrades) per-package with a verification step after each package, never repo-wide in one shot.
rule.rationale: Codemods accumulate silent failures across packages; a per-package run catches and fixes each failure before it compounds into a large-scale rollback.

content.prompt:
You are a stack auditor. Today is {{TODAY_ISO}}.
Inputs to read (do NOT edit): - package.json - tsconfig.json (and any tsconfig.*.json) - pyproject.toml - next.config.{js,ts,mjs} (if present) - .eslintrc* / eslint.config.* (if present) - README.md / AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md
Reference: skills/faion/knowledge/solo/dev/automation-tooling/best-practices-2026/methodology.xml
Produce a markdown report with these sections: 1. Detected versions (TS, React, Next, Python, ruff, eslint, node engine). 2. Gap table: rule -> current -> required -> severity (block | warn | info). 3. Migration plan as ordered SDD tasks with token estimates (no time estimates). 4. Risk assessment for global flag flips (TS strict, exactOptionalPropertyTypes). 5. AI-tooling section: which primary tool is documented, where guardrails live.
Constraints: - Do not edit any file. Output report only. - Quote exact line references for every gap. - If a rule is satisfied, mark it OK with the exact evidence line. - For Next.js 15 fetch caching, list every fetch() call with its file:line and current cache mode.

Goal: bring tsconfig.json to the 2026 strict baseline.
Steps: 1. Read current tsconfig.json. Diff against templates/tsconfig-strict-2026.json. 2. For each missing flag, decide: enable now OR add // @ts-strict-check per-file gradually. 3. If enabling globally, run npx tsc --noEmit and capture error count. 4. If error count > 50, revert global flag and switch to per-file mode; document the decision in .aidocs/decisions/<date>-tsconfig-strict.md. 5. If error count <= 50, fix all errors and commit. 6. Update CHANGELOG.md under [Unreleased]: "feat: tsconfig 2026 strict baseline".
Stop conditions: - npx tsc --noEmit must exit 0 before commit. - Pre-commit hook must pass.

template.purpose: TS 5 strict baseline with all 2026 recommended flags.
Python 3.13 + ruff + mypy strict baseline for pyproject.toml.

verify.step: Run npx tsc --noEmit on the project root; expect exit code 0 with all 2026 strict flags enabled.
Run npx eslint . --max-warnings=0; expect exit code 0.
Run ruff check . and ruff format --check .; both must exit 0.
Run grep -rn "'use client'" app/ src/ and verify each occurrence has a comment within 3 lines explaining the client-boundary reason.
Run grep -rEn "fetch\(" app/ and confirm every match either has cache: option, revalidate option, or an explicit // request-fresh: comment.
Run grep -rEn "from typing import (List|Dict|Optional|Tuple|Set|Union)" --include="*.py"; expect zero matches in src/ (test fixtures may keep legacy imports).
Run python -c "import sys; assert sys.version_info[:2] == (3, 13)"; expect exit code 0.
Open the latest AI-generated PR and confirm: (a) human review left at least one comment OR a documented review record, (b) diff size <= 200 lines OR PR was split.
Mutation-check at least one AI-generated test file: revert the function it tests and rerun; at least one assertion must fail.
Validate this methodology file: python3 scripts/validate-methodology-xml.py skills/faion/knowledge/solo/dev/automation-tooling/best-practices-2026/methodology.xml; expect PASS.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Two-phase load: (1) read the methodology to pick relevant sections; (2) read the referenced templates and apply. For audits, pre-load all sections; for targeted upgrades (e.g., "modernise TypeScript config"), load only the typescript-5-strict-configuration-2026-baseline section. Always cross-reference against the actual installed versions (package.json, pyproject.toml) before applying recommendations — the doc presumes current versions.

Recommended subagents: improver — natural fit: it audits a system, finds gaps, proposes upgrades; this hub is its rubric. /faion — executes the upgrade tasks once the gap list is produced. review skill — reviews the resulting modernisation PR.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: "2026" snapshot ages quickly: TypeScript 5.x, React 19, Next.js 15, Python 3.13 are 2025-Q4 reality. New majors (React 20 / Python 3.14 / TS 6) will break section claims; cross-check release notes.
AI-coding section conflates capabilities of Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code — recommendations may not reflect current pricing/UX.
section.warning: Hub-only read: an agent may confidently report recommendations from the TOC alone. Always require both the methodology and the relevant template files.
Stale "2026" claims: agent quotes "TS 5.x, React 19" as if frozen. After a Node/React major release, agent should consult release notes, not just the doc.
Codemod application: agent runs codemods (e.g., React 19) on the entire repo unattended; failures pile up silently. Run per-package with a verification step.
Python typing: agent mixes typing.List (legacy) with list[T] (PEP 585) in the same file; force from __future__ import annotations or commit fully to PEP 585.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: any change to tsconfig global strictness flags, eslint root config, or pyproject.toml lint set; these have repo-wide blast radius.
"Recent enough" trap: agent that hasn't been told the current date applies 2024 best practices to a 2026 codebase. Always anchor with "Today's date is YYYY-MM-DD" in the prompt.

list.item: "Currentness" is a maintenance practice, not a snapshot. Schedule a quarterly review against this hub; treat it as a rubric, not a constitution.
Adopt strict TS in new code first; convert legacy modules under // @ts-strict-check boundary or per-file pragmas; do not flip the global flag without budget.
React 19 server components: don't sprinkle 'use client' randomly; document a per-route policy (server-by-default, client-on-demand).
Pin Python at minor (>=3.13,<3.14) until next LTS-equivalent stabilises; agents using >=3.13 accept future majors and break compat.
AI-assisted dev: require diff-review on every AI-generated change; reject anything > 200 lines unreviewed. The doc is permissive; the team must be stricter.
For test generation by AI, run the original suite first to establish baseline; reject AI-added tests that don't fail when reverting the change (mutation-style sanity check).
Track DORA metrics + a small "AI-effectiveness" panel (PR cycle time pre/post AI tooling) — the Best Practices hub should pay back, not just feel good.
npm-check-updates (ncu) — detect outdated JS/TS deps. Install: npm i -g npm-check-updates.
pip-audit / pip list --outdated — Python dep currency. Install: pip install pip-audit.
cargo outdated — Rust dep currency. Install: cargo install cargo-outdated.
bun outdated — Bun-native, bundled with bun.
tsc --noEmit + tsconfig-strict-defaults — validate TS strictness, bundled.
ruff (Python) / eslint (JS) — 2026 default linters. Install: pip install ruff or npm i -D eslint.
jscodeshift, @react-codemod, @next/codemod — automated migrations.
Renovate / Dependabot — automated dep PRs to keep the 2026 baseline current.
GitHub Copilot / Cursor / Claude Code — AI coding tool selection slot; pick one primary.
SonarCloud / Snyk / Sentry — code-quality, security, perf observability baseline.
